skill.name: network-netcat
skill.description: Guidance for netcat and ncat network testing workflows, including connectivity checks, banner grabbing, port testing, file transfer, and offensive shell techniques. The source contains high-risk dual-use material that requires strict authorization controls.
skill.summary: Use netcat for authorized network testing while recognizing its high-risk offensive capabilities.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported extensive command execution, network, and filesystem patterns. Manual review confirms critical risk in SKILL.md because it provides actionable reverse shell, bind shell, persistence, payload delivery, pivoting, and data exfiltration workflows; several findings in template reference files are benign educational examples. No evidence found of prompt injection text in the reviewed files.

critical_findings.title: Actionable Reverse and Bind Shell Guidance
critical_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md gives step-by-step reverse shell, bind shell, PTY upgrade, and reconnection patterns. This enables unauthorized interactive command execution if copied outside an approved lab. Confidence 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The static shell execution findings are confirmed by multiple semantically aligned shell access sections, including Linux, Windows, PowerShell, and persistence-like reconnection examples.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static shell execution findings are confirmed by multiple semantically aligned shell access sections, including Linux, Windows, PowerShell, and reconnection examples.

critical_findings.title: Persistent Backdoor Setup Instructions
critical_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md describes creating a persistent netcat service, cron startup entry, and Windows scheduled task. These are durable access mechanisms and match malicious persistence behavior. Confidence 0.98. Confidence reasoning: The cited section explicitly labels the pattern as a persistent backdoor and provides platform-specific persistence mechanisms.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section explicitly labels the pattern as a persistent backdoor and provides platform-specific persistence mechanisms.

critical_findings.title: Data Exfiltration Workflow
critical_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md includes a data exfiltration pattern for sensitive system files, database dumps, and compressed directories over netcat. This is direct unauthorized data transfer guidance. Confidence 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The section title and examples align with exfiltration behavior, including sensitive file and database movement to an attacker-controlled listener.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section title and examples align with exfiltration behavior, including sensitive file and database movement to an attacker-controlled listener.

high_findings.title: Post-Exploitation Payload Delivery and Metasploit Use
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md explains staged payload delivery and using netcat through Metasploit sessions. This materially supports post-exploitation control of compromised systems. Confidence 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The source names compromised hosts, payload stages, and Metasploit session actions, which confirms offensive post-exploitation context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The source names compromised hosts, payload stages, and Metasploit session actions, which confirms offensive post-exploitation context.

high_findings.title: Relay, Pivot, and Covert Communication Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md covers relays through compromised hosts, internal network pivoting, and simple covert communication channels. These techniques can bypass segmentation and monitoring controls. Confidence 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The guidance explicitly describes relay and pivot use through compromised infrastructure, not only benign connectivity testing.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly describes relay and pivot use through compromised infrastructure, not only benign connectivity testing.

medium_findings.title: Plain Netcat File Transfer Patterns
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md documents file and archive transfer over netcat, including checksum verification and SSL variants. File transfer is legitimate in labs but creates data movement risk when paired with shell and exfiltration sections. Confidence 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The examples are dual-use and can be benign, but their placement inside an offensive workflow increases risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are dual-use and can be benign, but their placement inside an offensive workflow increases risk.

medium_findings.title: CI Template Pipe-to-Shell Installer
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: assets/ci-config-template.yml downloads an installer script and pipes it to a shell. This is a supply-chain risk because remote content executes in CI without local verification. Confidence 0.84. Confidence reasoning: The pattern is directly present in a CI pipeline template, although it appears to be illustrative rather than hidden behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in a CI pipeline template, although it appears to be illustrative rather than hidden behavior.

low_findings.title: Benign Security Rule Template Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Many env_access, weak crypto, hardcoded secret, and URL findings in assets/rule-template.yaml are examples inside a security rule template. They describe detection and remediation patterns, not executable skill behavior. Confidence 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The file is structured as a rule template with vulnerable and fixed examples, making these detections expected educational content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is structured as a rule template with vulnerable and fixed examples, making these detections expected educational content.

low_findings.title: Benign Reference Document Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: XSS, SQL injection, environment variable, and API key detections in references/EXAMPLE.md are documentation examples for security review and remediation. They are not active scripts. Confidence 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The reference file labels the snippets as vulnerable or fixed examples for training, which reduces execution risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference file labels the snippets as vulnerable or fixed examples for training, which reduces execution risk.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE CHECK: No evidence found of text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override the audit, claim pre-approval, or skip security analysis. Confidence 0.80. Confidence reasoning: Targeted search across SKILL.md, assets, and references found no matching prompt-injection indicators, though this does not reduce the confirmed offensive-content risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search across SKILL.md, assets, and references found no matching prompt-injection indicators, though this does not reduce the confirmed offensive-content risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive Shell Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed dangerous pattern: the skill teaches netcat and PowerShell shell access patterns for remote command execution. Confidence 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The examples are operational shell access patterns across several platforms.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are operational shell access patterns across several platforms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistence Mechanism Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed dangerous pattern: the skill describes system service, cron, and scheduled task persistence for a netcat backdoor. Confidence 0.98. Confidence reasoning: The pattern directly maps to persistence techniques and is named as a backdoor in the source.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern directly maps to persistence techniques and is named as a backdoor in the source.

dangerous_patterns.title: Exfiltration Over Network Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed dangerous pattern: the skill moves sensitive files, database dumps, and archives over netcat to a remote listener. Confidence 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The section explicitly frames these examples as data exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly frames these examples as data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Pipe-to-Shell Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed dangerous pattern: a CI template executes a remote installer script through the shell, creating supply-chain exposure. Confidence 0.84. Confidence reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is present in CI context, but it appears as a template rather than hidden execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is present in CI context, but it appears as a template rather than hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Security teams need clear review of netcat workflows before using them in controlled environments. This skill explains network testing patterns, but its offensive shell and exfiltration guidance makes it unsafe for open marketplace publishing.
actual_capabilities: Explains TCP and UDP connectivity checks with netcat and ncat.
Documents banner grabbing and simple port availability testing.
Covers file transfer workflows and verification concepts.
Describes reverse shell, bind shell, and PTY upgrade workflows.
Includes relay, pivoting, persistence, and post-exploitation examples.
Lists defensive monitoring considerations for netcat activity.
limitations: The source contains actionable offensive content that can enable unauthorized access.
It does not enforce authorization, scope validation, or technical safety controls.
Netcat scanning is slower and less complete than dedicated network scanners.
Several examples require tools or privileges that may not exist on target systems.

use_cases.title: Authorized Connectivity Review
use_cases.description: Review permitted netcat connectivity checks for known in-scope hosts and document expected results.

use_cases.title: Security Content Audit
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether netcat guidance crosses from defensive testing into unsafe offensive instruction.

use_cases.title: Detection Planning
use_cases.description: Identify netcat behaviors that should trigger process, network, and command-line monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Scope Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm allowed use before any network test.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this planned netcat connectivity test for authorization, scope, and logging requirements. Do not provide shell access or bypass instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Connectivity Result Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me summarize netcat connectivity and banner results for these approved hosts. Focus on findings, uncertainty, and next defensive steps.

prompt_templates.title: Detection Controls
prompt_templates.scenario: Build monitoring guidance for netcat activity.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a defensive monitoring checklist for netcat and ncat use across endpoints and network logs. Exclude offensive execution steps.

prompt_templates.title: Marketplace Risk Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this netcat skill for marketplace publication risk. Separate benign testing guidance from shell access, persistence, pivoting, and exfiltration content.

output_examples.input: Review a proposed netcat connectivity test for a production subnet.
output_examples.output: The response identifies required written authorization, target scope, approved time window, logging plan, and safe stopping conditions.

output_examples.input: Summarize allowed banner grabbing results for three internal services.
output_examples.output: The response groups services by observed banner, notes missing responses, and recommends defensive validation without adding exploit steps.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether this skill should be published.
output_examples.output: The response flags reverse shells, persistence, exfiltration, and pivoting as publication blockers, while noting benign connectivity sections.

best_practices: Use this material only for assets covered by written authorization and a defined test scope.
Prefer defensive summaries, detection guidance, and connectivity validation over shell access workflows.
Log each approved test with source, destination, time, owner, and cleanup confirmation.
anti_patterns: Do not use netcat to establish shell access on systems without explicit written approval.
Do not transfer sensitive files or database exports through ad hoc listeners.
Do not create services, cron jobs, or scheduled tasks that maintain unauthorized access.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish as submitted?
faq.answer: No. The audit confirms critical offensive workflows that are unsafe for open marketplace publication.

faq.question: Are all static findings true positives?
faq.answer: No. Several template and reference examples are benign security training content, but the main skill has confirmed critical risk.

faq.question: Does the skill contain executable malware?
faq.answer: No evidence found of packaged malware. The risk comes from actionable instructions for shell access, persistence, and exfiltration.

faq.question: Can netcat be used legitimately?
faq.answer: Yes. Netcat can support authorized connectivity testing, banner checks, and troubleshooting within a clearly approved scope.

faq.question: What would reduce the publication risk?
faq.answer: Remove shell access, persistence, exfiltration, pivoting, and post-exploitation instructions. Keep defensive and connectivity-only guidance.

faq.question: What monitoring should teams consider?
faq.answer: Monitor nc and ncat process execution, unusual outbound connections, command-line shell flags, and unexpected listeners.
